The original construction of the Charter with Section 33 was a nicely balanced approach giving both the Courts and Parliament (and Legislatures) the authority to interpret individual rights. Courts interpret rights based on the law but it is the legislatures that must truly balance individual rights with broader public policy. The fact that the Federal Parliament has never used Section 33 over the past 35 years is the real problem. Liberal governments have consistently abdicated their responsibility on a number of files by not using Parliament's override authority. Conservative governments have been gutless too.  There is pent up demand for our elected leaders to begin assuming their responsibility and entering the conversation about rights and freedom. I for one am delighted to see Conservative leaders broaching the idea of using Section 33. It is long overdue.

There is a huge gaping hole in this proposal, i.e., on the basis of what information does the individual who extrapolates a personality profile from social media expression confirm that his/her extrapolation is correct?

Another item to consider here is that perhaps we should not ignore that people who use social media as a deeply integrated part of their life are a unique personality-profile group in and of themselves.  This in much the same way that a standard deviation should probably be factored when concluding where--in relation to "normal"--anyone stands who takes their personal or family problems into public exposure on a TV program or "reality" TV. Economics is a particularly problematic case, partly because of the idea - propagated by neoclassical economists themselves since about 1900 - that it is like the sciences, when in many important ways it is not. The EC projections were nothing more than a model - to address these known imprecisions, they take the data they do have (current polling and past election patterns) and run thousands of simulated elections, varying the parameters slightly with each mock run. The models predicted a Trump win about 10-20% of the time, an uncommon but not rare event. 

He won all three of his threshold states (MI, PA, WI) by under 1% of the vote, well below the sensitivity of polling.  This is not inconsistent with it being something of a statistical fluke - you wouldn't need to change a lot of votes (about 85,000, circa 1/4000 of America's population) to change the total outcome. .

It took Hurricane Hazel to shock people in Southern Ontario into the insight that building your home on a flood plain was not a good decision. And so conservation authorities were organized around the river systems so that when (not if) the next "storm of the century" came the damage could be limited. The problem, as I see it, is that the protective measures anticipated a certain level of urban growth, not the explosive expansion of suburbs onto what was once the prime agricultural land of southern Ontario. All those suburbs have roads and roofs which collect the downpour and deliver it directly into the headwaters of the river systems, resulting in greater than anticipated flow further downstream. Consider the new suburbs south of Caledon Mountain - and the glut of water they add to the original streams after a deluge.  The sensible solution would be to stop the expansion of suburbs onto farmland - but good luck getting that past the politicians and the developer lobby.

I agree wholeheartedly with this editorial. There seems to be a headlong rush to the gross receipts tax to the exclusion of other far less contentious (and less regressive) solutions. Raise the corporate income tax, for heavens sakes. I believe business would support a reasonable increase. It is not lost on business that their total tax burden here is low. But the gross receipts tax is by far the least popular tax with business, and with good reason: it is unrelated to profits or profit margins, and the single-rate structure in the current proposal is massively unfair, taxing some business types far more heavily than others.

And the gross receipts tax is no panacea.  It is definitely not immune to economic cycles: corporate sales plunge just like corporate profits during recessions.

The gross receipts tax is used by only a handful of states and some have tried it and repealed it. Oregon shouldn't touch it. It won't be approved, and we'll be back where we started.

We have very sensible safety requirements at the federal and provincial levels. These requirements are of little use without a complementary effort at verification and enforcement.

My interest in this topic has nothing to do with flooding: it has to do with the ongoing pattern of allowing the development of 'high-consequence' sites next to transmission pipelines and freight railways. These sites include large geriatric facilities, schools, 22-storey high-rises, a major transit hub, etc. 

Ontario has a specific requirement that "A minimum setback of 200 metres shall be maintained from the centerline of pipelines to institutions where rapid evacuation may be difficult..." (TSSA, 1998).
